original_abstract: The present paper outlines generally adopted and universally applied methodological assumptions of the evaluation of road accidents costs in the member states of the European Union. The valuation of road occurrences is recommended by means of EU directives as an essential element of safety processes of the road traffic. However, the level of data accessibility in individual member states has an effect on valuation details and the subjective scope of component categories of road accident unit costs. That is why the value of road accident costs is one of the least authoritative parameters because it is really difficult to compare its values coming from separate countries. The most significant evaluation element of accident costs has exactly humanistic dimension because it is about the valuation of non-material effects in the form of pain, sufferings, and finally the social exclusion. The paper elaborates essential problems related to the evaluation of basic categories of road accident costs (selected with regard to the data accessibility) and presents a couple of selected crucial categories of cost valuation in a social context including the value of human life.
humanized_abstract: The paper presents generally accepted and most commonly used assumptions in evaluating the costs of road accidents in the European Union (EU) member states. In accordance with EU directives, assessing the value of road accidents is one of the key elements of safety inspections in road traffic. However, due to differences in the availability of data in individual countries, assumptions used for valuation and the scope of components in the unit cost of road accidents are different. Therefore, the value of road accidents is one of the least significant parameters, due to large differences in their values between countries. The most important parameter of road accident costs has a humanistic character, because it relates to assessing the value of non-material damage to health, such as physical suffering, pain and social isolation. The paper deals with the key problems that arise when evaluating individual components of road accident costs (chosen in relation to the availability of data), as well as with the valuation of selected important components of the cost of road accidents in a social context, including the value of human life.
